title: Diversity and social change
summary: 9699 — changing family structures, diversity, and demographic trends.
learningObjectives: Describe trends in marriage, divorce, and cohabitation.
Explain increasing family diversity and its causes.
Analyse same-sex families, lone-parent, and reconstituted families.
Evaluate whether the family is in decline.

simpleExplanation.title: Families aren't all the same anymore
simpleExplanation.summary: Rising divorce, cohabitation, and diverse household types mean 'the family' is now a range of arrangements shaped by law, economics, and culture.
steps: Chester's neo-conventional family vs Rapoport diversity.

flashcards.back: Dual-earner, child-centred — still dominant despite diversity.
Partners with children from previous relationships — complex relationships.
Divorce, death, choice, unplanned pregnancy — avoid stereotyping.
Legal recognition; research shows children outcomes similar when supported.
New Right sees moral decline; postmodernists see positive choice/diversity.

sections.content: **Family diversity** (3.2) uses **official statistics** and **postmodern theory**. Quantify change where possible (marriage/divorce rates, cohabitation).

sections.question: Assess the view that the family has become more diverse in modern society. [15 marks]
sections.solution:
**Diversity evidence:** Rising **cohabitation**, **divorce**, **lone-parent**, **same-sex** families; Rapoport's **five diversities**; ethnic variation in family structure.

**Continuity:** Chester — **neo-conventional** dual-earner family still common; many still marry; **class** shapes experience of diversity.

**Causes:** Secularisation, feminism, welfare state, legal change, individualisation (Beck).

**Evaluation:** **Structural diversity increased** but **not all forms equally accepted** — stigma on lone parents (Murray); **material inequality** persists within diverse types.

**Conclusion:** More **types** of family, but debate over whether this is **liberating choice** or **instability**.
